headline: Quebec Firm Cuts Gold Mining's Environmental Toll
meta_description: A Quebec chemical firm has developed a cyanide-free method to extract gold, eliminating toxic arsenic through an innovative glass process.
summary_short: Dundee Sustainable Technologies has created a safer way to mine gold without cyanide, converting arsenic into inert glass instead of toxic waste.
body:
When Freegold Ventures tested a new gold extraction method on ore from Alaska's Golden Summit project, the results told a compelling story about how chemistry can reshape mining's environmental footprint.

The process, called GlassLock and developed by Quebec's Dundee Sustainable Technologies, replaced cyanide leaching with sodium hypochlorite and sodium hypobromite—chemicals that work at room temperature in just 2 hours instead of the traditional 36-hour cyanide method. But the real breakthrough lies in what happens to arsenic, one of mining's most stubborn environmental problems.

Instead of ending up in tailings ponds—the manmade lakes that can rupture during storms or earthquakes—the arsenic is transformed into inert glass by mixing it with silica, recycled glass, and hematite. In testing, the process recovered 95% of gold while isolating 98% of arsenic, reducing its toxicity from 7% to just 0.17%.

For mining companies already navigating 8 to 20 years of environmental permitting in North America, this matters enormously. Removing the need for tailings ponds eliminates a persistent regulatory headache while actually protecting the ecosystems surrounding mining operations. It's an example of how cleaner technology can make responsible resource extraction work better—for both industry and the environment.
